zh:
印加人把自己的国家称作“塔万廷苏尤”——四方之地，以库斯科为脐心；这个13世纪立足库斯科谷地的政权，自15世纪中叶起不到百年，就把安第斯山脉纵向近四千公里的高原、峡谷与海岸收入一个帝国。他们的神话也随之分层：国家层面是太阳神因蒂的官方崇拜，印加王以“太阳之子”的身份统治，库斯科的科里坎查神庙金板覆墙；更古老的底层则是安第斯本土的大地与山岳信仰——大地母神帕查妈妈、居于雪峰的阿普山神、蛰伏深湖的巨蛇阿马鲁，以及在的的喀喀湖畔创造日月与人类的维拉科查。世界分作三层：天上的“哈南帕查”、人间的“凯帕查”与地下的“乌库帕查”，圣石、泉眼、山口与湖泊皆可为“瓦卡”，即受祭的圣所。

印加人没有文字，结绳记事的“基普”至今无法通读，因此这套神话全靠殖民时代的纸笔存世：贝坦索斯《印加人的总述与叙事》（1551）、萨米恩托《印加史》（1572）、莫利纳的祭仪记述、混血王孙加西拉索的《印卡王室述评》（1609）、原住民编年史家瓜曼·波马附有近四百幅插图的《新编年史与善政》（约1615），以及用克丘亚语写成的《瓦罗奇里手稿》（约1608）。这些文本无一不经过西班牙人或教会之手，读印加神话，始终要隔着征服者的墨水辨认湖底的原貌。

这也是一套没有死去的神话。帕查妈妈至今在八月受祭，山口的石堆仍在加高，奥桑加特山下的雪星节每年聚起数万朝圣者；被斩首的印加王在口传的“印卡里”传说里等待身首重合、天地翻转的一日。从的的喀喀湖的创世圣岩到库斯科重新排演的太阳节，这份图鉴里的许多条目，至今仍有人献祭、有人讲述。
en:
The Incas called their realm Tawantinsuyu, the Four Quarters Together, with Cusco as its navel; a Cusco-valley polity from the thirteenth century, it took less than a hundred years from the mid-fifteenth to draw nearly four thousand kilometers of Andean highland, canyon and coast into a single empire. Its mythology is layered the same way. Above sat the official cult of the sun god Inti, whose son the Inca king claimed to be, worshipped in the gold-sheeted Coricancha of Cusco; beneath it lay the far older native religion of earth and mountain — Pachamama the earth mother, the apu lords dwelling in the snowpeaks, the great serpent amaru asleep in deep lakes, and Viracocha, who made sun, moon and humankind at Lake Titicaca. The world had three tiers: Hanan Pacha above, Kay Pacha here, Ukhu Pacha below, and any stone, spring, pass or lake could be a huaca, a shrine owed offerings.

The Incas had no writing, and the knotted cords of the quipu still cannot be read as text, so this mythology survives entirely through colonial pens: Betanzos's Suma y narración de los Incas (1551), Sarmiento's Historia de los Incas (1572), Molina's account of the rites, the Royal Commentaries (1609) of Garcilaso — himself of Inca royal blood on his mother's side — the Nueva corónica of the indigenous chronicler Guaman Poma (c. 1615) with its nearly four hundred drawings, and the Quechua-language Huarochirí Manuscript (c. 1608). Every one of these texts passed through Spanish or churchly hands; reading Inca myth means peering at the lake bed through the conquerors' ink.

It is also a mythology that never died. Pachamama still receives her offerings each August, the cairns at the high passes keep growing, and tens of thousands of pilgrims gather every year for the Snow Star festival below Ausangate; in the oral legend of Inkarri, the beheaded Inca king waits underground for the day his body is whole and the world turns over. From the rock of creation in Lake Titicaca to the sun festival restaged in Cusco, many of the entries here describe rites still performed and stories still told.
